title: Core, Java, Python, Node, Go: Add client-wide circuit breaker
body:
### Summary

Add a client-wide circuit breaker that detects sustained error rates in the Rust core and rejects requests at the FFI boundary before threads park. Opt-in, disabled by default. All clients get config exposure; Java additionally performs a synchronous pre-check to prevent thread explosion under `managedBlock()`.

### Issue link

Closes #5996

### Features / Behaviour Changes

- New `ClientCircuitBreakerConfiguration` config and `CircuitBreakerException/CircuitBreakerError` class in all clients (Java, Python, Node, Go)
- When enabled: CB tracks error rate in a sliding window, trips when threshold exceeded, rejects requests instantly while open, recovers automatically via optimistic HalfOpen with consecutive success validation
- Java: synchronous `is_healthy()` check at JNI boundary (same location as inflight check)
- Error reporting and CB pre-check centralized in Rust core's `send_command` so all clients benefit automatically
- Logging: error type breakdown on trip, rate-limited rejection count while open, recovery duration

### Implementation

- `glide-core/src/client/circuit_breaker.rs` — State machine (Closed/Open/HalfOpen), sliding window with 10K cap, failure rate threshold, `try_write()` for non-blocking health check, input validation
- `glide-core/src/client/mod.rs` — CB wired into Client struct, result reporting after `send_command`, pre-check rejection, `is_healthy()` exposed
- `glide-core/src/errors.rs` — `CircuitBreakerOpen` error type classification
- `glide-core/src/protobuf/response.proto` — `CircuitBreakerOpen = 4` in `RequestErrorType`
- `glide-core/src/protobuf/connection_request.proto` — `ClientCircuitBreakerConfig` message
- `glide-core/README.md` — Developer documentation for CB
- `java/src/lib.rs` — Sync pre-check at both JNI entry points with parameterized error code
- `java/src/jni_client.rs` — `complete_error_sync` accepts error code parameter
- `java/client/.../CircuitBreakerException.java` — Dedicated exception type
- `java/client/.../ClientCircuitBreakerConfiguration.java` + `ConnectionManager.java` — Config + validation + protobuf serialization
- Python: `CircuitBreakerError`, config class with validation, dispatch in `get_request_error_class()`
- Node: `CircuitBreakerError`, config interface with `ConfigurationError` validation, dispatch in `getRequestErrorClass()`
- Go: `CircuitBreakerError` struct, dispatch via C enum, config with validation

### Testing

- 10 Rust unit tests (state machine, concurrency, window eviction, validation)
- Java integration test (config accepted, normal operations unaffected)
- E2E docker benchmark: consistent results across runs showing CB reduces slow errors by ~50% and converts them to fast rejections
- Manual smoke tests passing for all 4 clients (Java, Python, Node, Go)

### Follow-up Work

- Expose CB config to remaining clients (valkey-glide-csharp, valkey-glide-php, valkey-glide-ruby)
- Documentation in valkey-glide-docs (CB configuration guide, best practices)
- Enhanced watchdog diagnostics ([#6044](https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey-glide/pull/6044)) — use timeout cause classification, inflight trends, and p99 data for smarter
tripping and recovery
- Per-shard CB granularity (future iteration based on data collected)

E2E tests over 12h (6h with and 6h without CB) showed significant reduction in slow (timeout) errors, keeping the core moving quickly:
```
cb-test-runner  | ========== CLIENT CIRCUIT BREAKER BENCHMARK ==========
cb-test-runner  | Metric                    Without CB      With CB
cb-test-runner  | ---                       ---             ---
cb-test-runner  | Successful ops/s          1               1
cb-test-runner  | Total errors              127650939       126795195
cb-test-runner  | Fast errors (<10ms)       110790317 (86.8%) 124262308 (98.0%)
cb-test-runner  | Slow errors (>=10ms)      16860622 (13.2%) 2532887 (2.0%)
cb-test-runner  | Peak pool size            605             613
cb-test-runner  | =====================================================
cb-test-runner  | Client Circuit Breaker Test PASSED
```

Also ran over 3 days and saw the same consistent results, with no issues.

### Checklist

- [x] This Pull Request is related to one issue.
- [x] Commit message has a detailed description of what changed and why.
- [x] Tests are added or updated.
- [x] CHANGELOG.md and documentation files are updated.
- [x] Linters have been run.
- [x] Destination branch is correct - main
- [x] Create merge commit if merging release branch into main, squash otherwise.
- [x] Make sure to update the documentation in the valkey-glide-docs repository if necessary

linked_issues.title: Add client-wide circuit breaker
linked_issues.body:
### Describe the feature

Add a per-client circuit breaker that detects when the GLIDE core is unhealthy and rejects requests synchronously at the FFI boundary before threads park. This is the MVP for circuit breaker functionality in GLIDE, with per-shard granularity as a potential follow-up based on data collected.

The CB lives in the Rust core as a single state machine per client instance. Each language client checks it synchronously before submitting a command (same location as the inflight limit check in [#5952](https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey-glide/pull/5952)).

`command() → [1. check inflight limit] → [2. check client CB] → [3. submit to Rust core via FFI]`

State machine: Closed → Open → HalfOpen → Closed

- **Closed:** Normal operation. Errors tracked in sliding window.
- **Open:** Core unhealthy. Requests rejected immediately at FFI boundary. Probes allowed after open_timeout.
- **HalfOpen:** Optimistic recovery. Allows all traffic through. Requires N consecutive successes before closing (grace period). Full traffic is allowed because blocking the entire client during recovery defeats the purpose.

What trips it:
- Aggregate error rate across all nodes exceeds `failure_rate_threshold` within `window_size`
- Only transport errors count (IoError, FatalSendError, FatalReceiveError, connection dropped)
- Timeouts: opt-in via `count_timeouts` (default false, to avoid false positives under Tokio starvation)
- Minimum error count (`min_errors`) must be reached before rate is evaluated

### Use Case

Under partial degradation or Tokio starvation, the client accumulates errors across nodes. Without a client-wide CB, `ForkJoinPool.managedBlock()` threads continue parking and the pool explodes. The per-client CB detects the sustained error pattern and rejects at the FFI boundary before threads park, preventing thread explosion and preserving application stability.

Also applies to standalone mode where per-node/per-shard CB would not apply.

### Proposed Solution

title: fix(android): coerce string width/height in ImageAssetOptions
body:
## Problem

On Android, `ImageAssetOptions.width/height` provided as strings like `"300"` were parsed with `optInt()`, which resolves string values to `0`. This caused oversized decode/scale operations that could crash older Android APIs (e.g., API 22) with "bitmap size exceeds 32 bits" error.

Fixes #6289

## Changes

### Android (`packages/ui-mobile-base/android/widgets/src/main/java/org/nativescript/widgets/Utils.java`)
- Added `parsePositiveInt(JSONObject, String)` method to safely parse numeric strings and numbers
- Updated `loadImageAsync` to use `parsePositiveInt` for `width`/`height` options instead of `optInt`
- Invalid values (non-numeric strings, negatives) fall back to `0` for default sizing

### TypeScript (`packages/core/image-asset/image-asset-common.ts`)
- Added `normalizeImageAssetOptions()` to coerce string dimensions to positive integers
- Added `toPositiveInt()` helper for robust string-to-number conversion
- Updated `ImageAssetBase.options` setter to normalize options
- Updated `getRequestedImageSize()` to use normalized options

### Tests (`packages/core/image-asset/__tests__/image-asset-options.spec.ts`)
- Added regression tests for string width/height coercion
- Added tests for invalid string fallback behavior
- Added tests for aspect ratio preservation with string dimensions

## Verification

- ✅ Core package builds successfully
- ✅ New regression tests pass
- ✅ Repro case with imagepicker using `{ width: "300", height: "300" }` no longer crashes on Android API 22
- ✅ String dimensions now behave identically to numeric inputs

## Backwards Compatibility

- Numeric `width`/`height` options unchanged
- Invalid string values now gracefully fall back to defaults instead of crashing
- All existing functionality preserved

title: Channels
body:
This PR brings the ability to create custom notification channels from javascript. This is how to use it:
```javascript
import {Notifications} from 'react-native-notifications';

Notifications.setNotificationChannel({
    channelId: 'my-channel',
    name: 'My Channel',
    importance: 5,
    description: 'My Description',
    enableLights: true,
    enableVibration: true,
    // groupId: 'your-group',
    showBadge: true,
    soundFile: 'custom_sound.mp3',  // place this in <project_root>/android/app/src/main/res/raw/custom_sound.mp3
    vibrationPattern: [200, 1000, 500, 1000, 500],
})
```

I've also fixed a bug (I think it is a bug?) where the notification would be shown in the drawer if it is received while the app is opened (the changes in `src/main/java/com/wix/reactnativenotifications/core/notification/PushNotification.java`). I think this is a bug, because with the react-native-firebase v5 I've never had this behaviour. Also the notification was displayed wrong (the title and body were missing).

This may be related to #488 and #80

This should be the API call to FCM to send a notification (I think this is the legacy API version, you can use this one as a reference if you use the new API version):
- url: https://fcm.googleapis.com/fcm/send
- method: `POST`
- headers (you can find the authorization key in your firebase console -> project settings -> cloud messaging -> server key):
```json
{
    "Authorization": "key=AAA...."
}
```
- body:
```json
{
    "to": "<fcm_token>",
    "priority": "high",
    "notification": {
        "title": "Title",
        "body": "You have received a new notification yayyy :) :) :)",
        "android_channel_id": "my-channel",
        "badge": "0",
        "sound": "custom_sound.mp3"
    },
    "data": {
        "some": "data here"
    }
}
```

There are 4 ways to use this PR in case you want to test it or actually use it:
1. Using [patch-package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/patch-package) (**the recommended way**):
    - checkout this branch in your **node_modules/react-native-notifications**
    - run `npm run build`
    - use patch-package and commit your changes to git (don't forget to add a postinstall to package.json as it says in the patch-package guide)
2. Forking my branch:
    - run `npm run build`
    - remove `lib/dist/` from .gitignore
    - commit to git
    - use your fork in package.json
3. Same as 2. but you can directly fork my release branch found [here](https://github.com/alexbuicescu/react-native-notifications/tree/channels-release)
3. Using my release branch found [here](https://github.com/alexbuicescu/react-native-notifications/tree/channels-release) (I **do not recommend** this one, as there is a possibility to delete the repo).

title: Client-side caching: add `serverAssisted` config and `CLIENT TRACKINGINFO` command (Node, Python, Go, Java)
body:
### Summary

Implements client-side caching phase 2 across all four language clients (Java, Python, Node.js, Go). Adds the `serverAssisted` configuration field to enable server-assisted invalidation via `CLIENT TRACKING ON BCAST`, and implements the `CLIENT TRACKINGINFO` diagnostic command on both standalone and cluster clients.

### Issue link

This Pull Request is linked to issue: [Client-Side Caching Phase 2](https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey-glide/issues/5961)
Closes #5961

### Features / Behaviour Changes

- **Server-assisted caching config**: New `serverAssisted` / `server_assisted` / `ServerAssisted` field on `ClientSideCache` config (defaults to `false`). When enabled with RESP3, glide-core sends `CLIENT TRACKING ON BCAST` during connection setup, enabling push-based invalidation.
- **`CLIENT TRACKINGINFO` command**: Returns tracking state diagnostics (`flags`, `redirect`, `prefixes`) on all clients. Cluster clients support both single-node (default) and multi-node routing.
- **Java batch removal**: Removed `clientTrackingInfo` from `BaseBatch` per the "no batch support" policy for connection-management diagnostics.

### Implementation

**Configuration (all clients)**
- Added `serverAssisted` field to each client's `ClientSideCache` config type
- Protobuf serialization passes the field through to glide-core (field already existed in the proto schema from PR #5962)

**Response type**
- `ClientTrackingInfo` class/struct/dataclass with `flags` (Set/slice), `redirect` (int), `prefixes` (Set/slice)
- Language-appropriate parser functions convert the raw map response from glide-core

**Command implementation pattern**
- Standalone: execute command, parse single response
- Cluster: default routes to random node (single response); multi-node route returns per-node map of parsed responses

**Cross-client alignment**
- Consistent documentation wording across all languages
- Shared `assertClientTrackingInfo(info, on)` test helper pattern in every client's test suite

linked_issues.title: Implement client-side caching Phase 2: server-assisted invalidation via CLIENT TRACKING
linked_issues.body:
### Summary

Phase 1 of client-side caching (TTL-based) is complete across all language clients. This issue tracks Phase 2: server-assisted cache invalidation using `CLIENT TRACKING ON BCAST`.

When `server_assisted=true` is configured in `ClientSideCache`, glide automatically:
- Sends `CLIENT TRACKING ON BCAST` during connection setup (RESP3 required)
- Listens for `__redis__:invalidate` push messages from the server
- Evicts affected keys from the local cache on invalidation
- Re-enables tracking on reconnect
- Flushes the entire cache on nil invalidation (server dropped tracking data)

Also adds `CLIENT TRACKINGINFO` as a diagnostic command across all language clients.

### Scope

- glide-core (Rust): core infrastructure
- Python
- Node.js
- Java
- Go

### References

- Phase 1 implementation: existing `ClientSideCache` config in all clients
- Valkey docs: https://valkey.io/topics/client-side-caching/

title: Add MEMORY commands, remove LATENCY/MEMORY batch support (Java, Python, Node, Go)
body:
### Summary

1. Add `MEMORY DOCTOR`, `MEMORY MALLOC-STATS`, `MEMORY PURGE`, and `MEMORY STATS` command support across all GLIDE clients (Java, Python, Node.js, Go).
2. Remove `LATENCY` and `MEMORY` commands from batch/pipeline APIs across all clients.
3. Remove the `LatencyEntry`/`LatencyEventInfo` typed response classes from Java (replacing them with raw `Object[][]` arrays for consistency with batch removal).

### Issue link

Closes #6286

### Features / Behaviour Changes

| Command | Standalone API | Cluster API |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `MEMORY DOCTOR` | Returns diagnostic string report | Returns per-node results via `ClusterValue`, with optional route |
| `MEMORY MALLOC-STATS` | Returns allocator stats string | Returns per-node results via `ClusterValue`, with optional route |
| `MEMORY PURGE` | Returns `"OK"` | Returns `"OK"`, with optional route |
| `MEMORY STATS` | Returns typed memory statistics object | Returns per-node results via `ClusterValue`, with optional route |

All commands support optional cluster routing across all clients.

Other changes:

- `LATENCY HISTORY`, `LATENCY LATEST`, and `LATENCY RESET` are removed from batch/pipeline APIs (Java, Python, Node.js, Go).
- `MEMORY` commands are not added to batch/pipeline APIs.
- Java: `LatencyEntry` and `LatencyEventInfo` typed response classes are removed. `latencyHistory` and `latencyLatest` now return raw `Object[][]` arrays.

### Design Decisions

- For `MEMORY STATS`, all clients except Java return a typed memory stats object with per-database breakdown. For Java, the raw `Map` is returned, consistent with existing patterns in that language.
- In Python, `MEMORY DOCTOR` and `MEMORY MALLOC-STATS` return `str` instead of `bytes` since they produce human-readable reports.
- `cluster.links` and `functions.caches` fields (added in Redis 7.0) are treated as optional in `MEMORY STATS` responses, returning `None`/`undefined`/`nil` on Redis 6.2. This matches the pattern used for Valkey 8.0+ fields (`db.dict.rehashing.count`, etc.).
- Go uses `ReadRequiredValue` for required fields (returns error on missing/wrong type) and `ReadResult` for optional fields (returns nil `Result` when absent).

### Implementation

**Rust Core:**

- Added `MemoryStatsReturnType` to `value_conversion.rs` for proper type conversion of nested `db.<N>` map entries in `MEMORY STATS` responses.

**Go:**

- Refactored existing Go MEMORY commands (from #5986) for consistency with other clients.
- Added typed `MemoryStats` and `MemoryStatsDb` response types with `Result[int64]` for optional fields.
- Introduced `ReadRequiredValue` helper that returns errors on missing/mistyped fields (vs `ReadValue` which fails silently).
- Updated cluster client to support optional routing for all memory commands.

**Java:**

- Added `memoryDoctor`, `memoryMallocStats`, `memoryPurge`, `memoryStats` to `ServerManagementCommands` and `ServerManagementClusterCommands` interfaces.
- Implemented in `GlideClient` and `GlideClusterClient` with cluster routing support.
- Removed `LatencyEntry`/`LatencyEventInfo` classes; latency commands now return raw `Object[][]`.
- Added `ResponseMatcher` utility to `BatchTestUtilities` for flexible batch response validation.

**Node.js/TypeScript:**

- Added command creators and `MemoryStats` interface in `Commands.ts`.
- Implemented in `GlideClient` and `GlideClusterClient` with route parameter support.
- Added `parseMemoryStatsResponse` for typed response parsing.
- Extracted `convertAndParseClusterResponse` helper to reduce cluster response parsing duplication.
- Moved `parseLatencyHistoryResponse`/`parseLatencyLatestResponse` from `BaseClient` to `Commands.ts`.

**Python:**

- Added `MemoryStats` and `MemoryStatsDb` dataclasses in `glide_shared/commands/memory.py` with `_parse_memory_stats` helper.
- Implemented in both async and sync standalone/cluster clients.

### Limitations

:white_circle: None

### Testing

- **Java:** Unit tests (`GlideClientTest`, `GlideClusterClientTest`) and integration tests for standalone and cluster modes.
- **Python:** Async integration tests (`test_async_client.py`) covering standalone, cluster, and multi-node routing. All 32 tests pass.
- **Node.js:** Shared tests (`SharedTests.ts`) and cluster-specific tests (`GlideClusterClient.test.ts`).
- **Go:** Integration tests (`integTest/memory_commands_test.go`) and example tests. All 31 tests pass.

All tests verify response types, field presence, version-conditional fields (7.0+, 8.0+), and cluster routing behavior.

### Checklist

Before submitting the PR make sure the following are checked:

- [x] This Pull Request is related to one issue.

title: [bugfix&improve] Fix alert label persistence and improve custom label input
body:
## What's changed?

### Related Issue
Fixes #3901

### Overview
This PR fixes multiple issues in the alert definition label system, including UI display problems and incorrect persistence of system built-in labels. It improves both backend data correctness and frontend usability by clearly separating system labels from user-defined custom labels and enhancing the label selector experience.

---

### Backend Changes

#### Fix: Prevent System Built-in Labels from Being Persisted
**Problem**  
When creating or updating alert definitions, system built-in labels (such as alert mode and severity labels) were incorrectly saved to the `Label` table. These labels are auto-generated by the system and should not be persisted as user-defined labels, leading to data pollution and incorrect label management.

**Solution**
- Introduced `isSystemBuiltInLabel()` method in `AlertDefineServiceImpl` to identify system built-in labels
- Filtered out all system built-in labels before saving labels to the database
- Only persist user-defined custom labels when creating or updating alert definitions
- System built-in labels include:
  - `CommonConstants.ALERT_MODE_LABEL`
  - `CommonConstants.LABEL_ALERT_SEVERITY`

**Affected Files**
- `hertzbeat-alerter/src/main/java/org/apache/hertzbeat/alert/service/impl/AlertDefineServiceImpl.java`

#### Refactor: Enable Cross-Module Label Reuse
- Moved `LabelDao` and `LabelService` from `hertzbeat-manager` to `hertzbeat-base` module
- This refactor enables shared label persistence logic across modules and avoids duplicated implementations

#### Feature: Auto-persist New Custom Labels
- Added `saveNewCustomLabel()` method in `AlertDefineServiceImpl`
- Automatically persists newly created custom labels when alert definitions are created or updated

---

### Frontend Changes

#### Fix & Improvement: Alert Label Selector Component
**Problems**
- Auto-added labels from severity selection were not properly displayed in the UI
- Custom labels entered in one row could appear in dropdown options of other rows (cross-row pollution)
- Users were unable to reliably add free-text custom labels

**Solutions**
- Enabled free-text custom label input by setting `labelIsCustom: true` in the alert-setting component
- Introduced `activeSearchIndex` and `activeSearchType` to precisely track the currently active input field
- Optimized custom option display logic to ensure custom labels and values only appear in their corresponding rows
- Enhanced `writeValue()` to correctly extract and rebuild `labelKeys` and `labelMap` from existing alert definitions
  - Ensures system built-in labels (e.g. `alertMode`, `alert.severity`) are correctly displayed for selection but not persisted
- Improved `onKeyChange()` and `onValueChange()` to automatically add newly entered custom label keys and values to the option list for reuse
- Enhanced `onSearch()` to clear stale custom input values when the search field is cleared, preventing leftover dropdown pollution

**Affected Files**
- `web-app/src/app/shared/components/label-selector/label-selector.component.html`
- `web-app/src/app/shared/components/label-selector/label-selector.component.ts`

---

### Result
- System built-in labels are no longer persisted, ensuring clean and correct label data
- Auto-generated labels are properly displayed and selectable in the UI
- Users can add and reuse custom labels through free-text input
- Label selector behavior is isolated per row, eliminating cross-row side effects


## Screenshot
![20260106-010118](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a5ea1f55-169e-444b-9578-d8ec72462912)

title: feat(ui): upgrade Angular to v21
body:
## Summary

Upgrades Angular from v19 to v21, addressing the two critical issues from the [reverted first attempt](https://github.com/microcks/microcks/issues/2055#issuecomment-4396610667):

1. **Bundle size regression** (11.35 MB → now 3.25 MB, same as baseline)
2. **NG8107 warnings** (all resolved)

## Changes

### Dependency Updates
| Package | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| Angular core/cli/compiler | 19.2.20 | 21.2.12 |
| Angular CDK/Material | 19.2.19 | 21.2.10 |
| ngx-bootstrap | 19.0.2 | 21.2.0 |
| ng2-file-upload | 8.0.0 | 10.0.0 |
| TypeScript | 5.7.2 | 5.9.3 |
| Node.js (pom.xml) | v18.20.5 | v22.12.0 |

### Breaking Change Fixes (ngx-bootstrap v21)
- Removed all `.forRoot()` calls from module imports (`BsDropdownModule`, `PopoverModule`, `TooltipModule`, `ModalModule`)
- Added `BsDropdownModule` to standalone component imports where dropdown directives are used in templates (`ActionComponent`, `PaginationComponent`, `SortComponent`, `ToastNotificationComponent`)

### NG8107 Warning Fixes
Replaced unnecessary optional chaining (`?.`) with dot operator (`.`) in templates where Angular's type checker determines the left side cannot be `null` or `undefined`:
- `action.component.html`: `config?.moreActions` → `config.moreActions` (narrowed by parent `*ngIf` guard)
- `card.component.html`: `config?.subTitle` → `config.subTitle` (`config!` definite assignment)
- `card-action.component.html`: `action?.disabled` → `action.disabled` (narrowed by `||` short-circuit)
- `filter-fields.component.html`: `currentField?.type` / `currentField?.queries` → `currentField.type` / `currentField.queries` (getter returns non-optional type)
- `pagination.component.html`: `config?.pageSizeIncrements` → `config.pageSizeIncrements` (`config!` definite assignment)

### Template Fix
- Fixed unclosed `<div>` tag in `test-create.page.html`

## Bundle Size Verification

| Mode | Angular 19 (baseline) | Angular 21 (this PR) |
|------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Production | ~3.21 MB | ~3.25 MB ✅ |
| Development | ~7.61 MB | ~7.57 MB ✅ |

The esbuild-based `application` builder output confirms: `Application bundle generation complete`.

Closes #2055

title: feat(client): copy an API/query/JS object to another application (#41919)
body:
## Description Adds a **"Copy to application"** right-click option on actions (APIs/queries) and JS objects in the editor entity explorer. It opens a modal to pick a target **Workspace → Application → Page** (filtered to ones the user can edit) and copies the entity there. **TL;DR** — Reuses the existing partial export/import endpoints, so there are **no server changes**. The client exports the single entity via the partial-export endpoint, wraps the returned `ApplicationJson` as an in-memory `File`, and imports it into the chosen target via the partial-import endpoint. Datasource reconciliation and name-collision refactoring are handled server-side by the import. The user stays in the current editor and sees a success toast. ### Notable choices - Target-workspace applications are fetched into a dedicated `ui.copyEntityToApp` redux slice rather than `ui.selectedWorkspace.applications` (which reflects the current editor's workspace and must not be clobbered mid-session). - The orchestration saga lives in `ce/sagas` with an `ee/` passthrough stub for an EE override seam (git-branched apps), matching the `NavigationSagas` convention. - Client-side permission filtering is UX-only; the server re-authorizes every target id (`MANAGE_PAGES` on the page, edit on the app/workspace). ### Known v1 limitations (surfaced in the modal via an info callout) - Copying a JS object does not auto-include the separate API/query actions it references by name. - Datasource credentials are not copied — the target reuses a datasource by name or creates a new unconfigured one. Fixes #41919 ## Automation /ok-to-test tags="@tag.All" ### :mag: Cypress test results > [!TIP] > 🟢 🟢 🟢 All cypress tests have passed! 🎉 🎉 🎉 > Workflow run: > Commit: 580f4f82d1546514b5b835f7b277d720e5b73bc8 > Cypress dashboard. > Tags: `@tag.All` > Spec: >
Thu, 02 Jul 2026 14:02:09 UTC ## Communication Should the DevRel and Marketing teams inform users about this change? - [ ] Yes - [x] No ## Summary by CodeRabbit * **New Features** * Added “Copy to application” context menu options for both actions and JS objects. * Introduced a “Copy to application” modal to select a target workspace, application, and page. * Enabled cross-app copy with success/error notifications, including loading of eligible target applications and pages. * **Backend Improvements** * Improved name collision handling during partial imports by generating unique names for imported entities. * **Tests** * Added unit and UI tests for the modal, reducer state, saga export/import flow, and context-menu dispatch for both copy paths.

title: fix: keep channel ids numeric
body:
## What
- Treat `listChannels()` channel `id` as a numeric field across TypeScript, iOS, and Android.
- Return native channel IDs to JavaScript as numbers instead of stringifying them.
- Add iOS and Android regression tests for numeric IDs and string-ID rejection.
- Update the Maestro fake Capgo server channel fixture to return numeric ids.

## Why
- The backend returns numeric channel IDs. iOS modeled them as `String?`, so `JSONDecoder` failed before channels could be returned.
- Android and the CI fake server also needed to preserve the same numeric contract.
- Fixes #706.

## How
- Changed `ChannelInfo.id` to `number` in TypeScript and required `Int` on iOS.
- Added a strict Android list-channel parser that keeps JSON numbers and rejects non-numeric IDs.
- Kept the existing server error-object fallback for non-array responses.
- Changed fake Maestro channel fixtures from string ids to numeric ids.

## Testing
- `bun run fmt`
- `bun run test:ios -- -only-testing:CapacitorUpdaterPluginTests/ListChannelsTests`
- `cd android && ./gradlew testDebugUnitTest --tests ee.forgr.capacitor_updater.CapacitorUpdaterUnitTest.listChannelsResponseKeepsNumericChannelIds --tests ee.forgr.capacitor_updater.CapacitorUpdaterUnitTest.listChannelsResponseRejectsStringChannelIds`
- `CAPGO_MAESTRO_PORT=4192 ... curl http://127.0.0.1:4192/api/channel?scenario=manual-zip` confirmed numeric ids in the fake server response
- `bun run verify`

## Not Tested
- Did not call the live Capgo channel API; regression tests and the Maestro fake server use representative native response fixtures.

title: [ISSUE #15420] fix(ai): support lenient skill upload versions
body:
Please do not create a Pull Request without creating an issue first.

## What is the purpose of the change

This change improves Skill ZIP import behavior so short or non-standard uploaded versions from `SKILL.md`, `_meta.json`, or `targetVersion` do not block first-time or legacy uploads. The server now normalizes numeric short versions where possible, resolves a legal target draft consistently for precheck, single upload, and batch upload, and the next console shows when an uploaded version was converted.

## Brief changelog

- Add lenient Skill upload version candidate resolution for precheck, single upload, and batch upload.
- Normalize numeric short versions such as `3` and `2.3` to `3.0.0` and `2.3.0`.
- Generate `0.0.1` for new Skills with invalid versions and the next draft version for existing Skills without drafts.
- Preserve existing draft overwrite semantics so existing drafts are only overwritten when `overwrite=true`.
- Keep the raw uploaded version in precheck responses for converted versions.
- Clarify next console hints when a short uploaded version is normalized first and then bumped because the normalized version already exists.
- Update next console upload dialog text, parser coverage, locale strings, and built static assets.
- Add unit and OpenAPI IT coverage for short uploaded version normalization and invalid uploaded version fallback.

## Verifying this change

- `mvn -pl ai spotless:check`
- `mvn -pl ai -Dtest=SkillOperationServiceImplTest test`
- `mvn -pl test/openapi-test -DskipTests test-compile`
- `npm run test -- src/utils/__tests__/skillUploadParser.test.ts`
- `npx eslint src/pages/skillManagement/components/UploadSkillDialog.tsx`
- `npm run build`
- Attempted `mvn -pl test/openapi-test -Pintegration-test -DskipTests=false -Dit.test=SkillUploadAdminApiOpenApiITCase,SkillUploadConsoleApiOpenApiITCase verify`; the local environment returned `403 access denied / User not found` before reaching the changed assertions.

Follow this checklist to help us incorporate your contribution quickly and easily:

* [ ] Make sure there is a Github issue filed for the change (usually before you start working on it). Trivial changes like typos do not require a Github issue. Your pull request should address just this issue, without pulling in other changes - one PR resolves one issue.
* [ ] Format the pull request title like `[ISSUE #123] Fix UnknownException when host config not exist`. Each commit in the pull request should have a meaningful subject line and body.
* [ ] Write a pull request description that is detailed enough to understand what the pull request does, how, and why.
* [ ] Write necessary unit-test to verify your logic correction, more mock a little better when cross module dependency exist. If the new feature or significant change is committed, please remember to add integration-test in [test module](https://github.com/alibaba/nacos/tree/master/test).

linked_issues.title: Improve Skill ZIP upload handling for invalid versions
linked_issues.body:
## Problem

When importing a Skill ZIP, the version in SKILL.md, _meta.json, or the upload targetVersion can come from older tools or other ecosystems and may not match Nacos supported formats like x.y.z or vN.

Rejecting the upload directly is a poor experience, especially for first-time uploads and older consoles that still call the upload API without using precheck.

## Expected behavior

Skill ZIP upload should handle invalid uploaded versions consistently across precheck, single upload, and batch upload:

- For a new Skill, generate draft version 0.0.1.
- For an existing Skill without a draft, generate the next draft version from the current maximum version, for example online 1.0.0 plus uploaded version 1.0 creates draft 1.0.1.
- For an existing Skill with an editing draft, use the draft version and only overwrite it when overwrite=true.
- Precheck should keep the raw uploaded version in the response so the console can display that it was converted to the valid target version.

## Additional context

The next console should make this visible to users, for example: uploaded version 0.1 has been converted to valid version 0.0.1.

title: feat: add support for confidential model serving
body:
## Summary

Adds confidential model serving support to KServe, enabling encrypted ML model artifacts to be decrypted only inside Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) using hardware attestation.

### What this PR does

**Go (API, controller, webhook):**
- Adds `ConfidentialSpec` (`enabled`, `resourceId`) to `PredictorExtensionSpec` (v1beta1) and `LLMModelSpec` (v1alpha2)
- Validation: requires `storageUri` when enabled, warns on OCI URIs, validates `kbs:///<repo>/<type>/<tag>` resource ID format
- Controller propagates confidential annotations to pods
- Webhook mutator injects `CONFIDENTIAL_ENABLED` + `CONFIDENTIAL_RESOURCE_ID` env vars into the storage initializer init container
- LLMISVC controller applies the same env var injection directly

**Python (confidential storage initializer):**
- `SecretResolver` abstract interface with `CDHSecretResolver` implementation (Confidential Data Hub, local to TEE guest)
- `JWEDecryptor` for detecting and decrypting JWE-encrypted model files (RFC 7516, A256KW + A256GCM)
- Confidential decryption logic integrated into the unified storage initializer image (no separate image required)
- RATS-protocol agnostic: works with any KBS backend (Trustee, Intel Trust Authority, etc.)

### How it works

1. User sets `confidential: enabled: true` on their InferenceService or LLMInferenceService
2. KServe injects confidential env vars into the storage initializer init container
3. The confidential storage initializer downloads model artifacts (from S3, HF, GCS, etc.)
4. It detects JWE-encrypted files (by `.jwe` extension or JWE header)
5. It contacts the Confidential Data Hub (CDH) running locally inside the TEE guest
6. CDH performs hardware attestation with a Key Broker Service (KBS) and retrieves the decryption key
7. The model is decrypted in-place inside the TEE, never exposed outside

### Example

```yaml
apiVersion: serving.kserve.io/v1beta1
kind: InferenceService
metadata:
  name: my-confidential-model
spec:
  predictor:
    model:
      modelFormat:
        name: sklearn
      storageUri: "s3://bucket/encrypted-model"
      confidential:
        enabled: true
        resourceId: "kbs:///default/keys/model-key"
```

## Test plan

- [x] Go unit tests for validation (confidential enabled/disabled, OCI URI warning, resource ID format)
- [x] Go unit tests for webhook (env var injection)
- [x] Go unit tests for LLMISVC controller (confidential config application)
- [x] Go integration tests pass (111 specs for InferenceService controller)
- [x] Python unit tests for `SecretResolver`, `CDHSecretResolver`, `JWEDecryptor` (16 tests)
- [x] [E2E tested on ARO cluster with CoCo peer-pods](https://github.com/font/kserve/tree/coco-e2e-tests/hack/confidential-test) (SEV-SNP TEE):
  - Unencrypted OCI modelcar on CoCo peer-pod ([Known issue with encrypted OCI images using CRI-O](https://github.com/font/kserve/tree/coco-e2e-tests/hack/confidential-test#encrypted-oci-images-not-supported-on-openshift))
  - JWE-encrypted sklearn model in MinIO (S3) → CDH → KBS → decrypted inside TEE → inference verified
  - LLMInferenceService confidential plumbing (env var injection)

## Related

- Design [KEP: Standards-Based Confidential Model Serving](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1wrFE0flCEXUfW8YPwavNTjyc12DvnqBIVRQIbpF5e-U/edit?usp=sharing)
- CoCo (Confidential Containers): https://confidentialcontainers.org

Fixes #5080
author: font

linked_issues.title: Standards-Based Confidential Model Serving
linked_issues.body:
/kind feature

**Describe the solution you'd like**

This proposal introduces a hardware-rooted mechanism for protecting machine learning models at rest and in use. By extending the **KServe Storage Initializer** and leveraging **Modelcars**, we enable the decryption of models only within a verified **Trusted Execution Environment (TEE)**. The design relies on **OCI** `ocicrypt` and **JSON Web Encryption (JWE)** to remain agnostic of specific hardware vendors or Key Broker Services (KBS).

**Links to the design documents:**
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1wrFE0flCEXUfW8YPwavNTjyc12DvnqBIVRQIbpF5e-U/edit?usp=sharing

Per-URI CSC selection would require multiple init containers, which is a bigger change. I believe a single field at the PredictorExtensionSpec level is the correct scope for now.

title: enhance: support text_match_fuzzy (edit-distance) scalar filter
body:
issue: #50920
design doc: [docs/design-docs/design_docs/20260702-text_match_fuzzy.md](docs/design-docs/design_docs/20260702-text_match_fuzzy.md)

## What

Adds a scalar filter operator `text_match_fuzzy(field, "query", max_edit_distance=K)` for typo-tolerant (Levenshtein edit-distance) matching on analyzed VARCHAR fields that have a text-match index. Filter-only: it returns a boolean bitset, no scoring (scoring is tracked separately in #50921). `K` is an integer in `[0, 2]` (tantivy's fuzzy automaton hard cap); out-of-range is rejected.

## How

Mirrors the existing `text_match` / `phrase_match` path through every layer, reusing the term-dictionary FST and tantivy's `FuzzyTermQuery` (no new index, no new storage):

- Tantivy binding: `fuzzy_match_query` tokenizes the query with the field analyzer and ORs one `FuzzyTermQuery` per token; exposed via the `tantivy_fuzzy_match_query` C entry (cbindgen header regenerated).
- C++ index: `TextMatchIndex::FuzzyMatchQuery` plus the wrapper, mirroring `MatchQuery`.
- proto: `OpType.TextMatchFuzzy = 17` (next free value; 15/16 are already `InnerMatch`/`RegexMatch`). The edit distance rides in the existing `UnaryRangeExpr.extra_values`, like slop / min_should_match, so no new field is added.
- grammar + Go parser: a new `text_match_fuzzy(...)` rule and `VisitTextMatchFuzzy`, which validates `K` to `[0, 2]` and stores it in `extra_values`.
- C++ executor: routes the op to the text-index path (dispatch, exec-path, and offset-input guards) and calls `FuzzyMatchQuery`, with a `[0, 2]` re-check on the C++ side (the same way `PhraseMatch` validates slop) for plans not built through the parser.

## Tests

- Rust unit test: distance 0/1/2, fuzzy-vs-exact, multi-token OR, and the distance `K+1` exclusion boundary.
- C++ gtest (`TextMatch.FuzzyIndex`, `GrowingNaive`, `SealedNaive`): index-level typo matching and the `K+1` boundary, plus the executor + growing-segment path — a typo matches the same rows as the exact term, `not text_match_fuzzy(...)`, and the executor guard rejects out-of-range / missing `max_edit_distance`.
- Go parser tests: valid 0/1/2 produce the right op and extra value; a templated query preserves the distance; out-of-range / missing / non-integer / overflow / non-string field / match-not-enabled are all rejected. Existing `text_match` and `phrase_match` behavior is unchanged (full planparserv2 suite green).

## Notes

- `max_edit_distance` becomes a reserved keyword in filter expressions, consistent with `minimum_should_match` and `threshold`.
- Out of scope, left as follow-ups: scoring (#50921), prefix-fuzzy, ES-style `AUTO` fuzziness, and combining with `minimum_should_match`.

title: feat(setup): collect comm prefs during setup and validate email
body: Closes #10269 ## Change Description ### Background The setup flow currently uses two API calls: 1. `POST /setup_lakefs` migrates the database, creates the initial admin user, and returns one-time credentials. 2. `POST /setup_comm_prefs` saves email and communication preferences. This becomes risky once email validation is added to `setup_comm_prefs`. If `setup_lakefs` succeeds but `setup_comm_prefs` rejects the email, the credentials have already been created but have not been shown yet. If the user refreshes or leaves the page, those one-time credentials can be lost. ### Approach This PR extends `POST /setup_lakefs` to accept communication preferences and validate the email during setup. The WebUI now sends username and comm prefs in the same request. This approach was chosen because it fixes the credential-loss failure mode with the smallest change: - email is validated before irreversible setup work - comm prefs are saved in the same request - the setup flow stays simple - no new endpoint is needed If validation fails, setup returns `400` and nothing irreversible has happened. Backward compatibility is preserved because the new fields are optional. Existing callers can continue sending only `username`. In that case, setup completes without comm prefs, `comm_prefs_missing` is set to `true`, and the WebUI continues the existing follow-up flow by calling `/setup_comm_prefs` after initialization. #### Email validation checks Email validation now applies to both `/setup_lakefs` and `/setup_comm_prefs`. Checks performed: - format validation using `ParseAddress()`, - known public suffix validation using `golang.org/x/net/publicsuffix` to reject made-up TLDs, - disposable domain detection, and - free-provider detection. > [!NOTE] > [`github.com/AfterShip/email-verifier`](https://github.com/AfterShip/email-verifier) is used for disposable and free-domain classification. We only use its built-in classification lists, so validation stays local and fast. > This library was chosen to avoid maintaining our own domain lists in this PR. If we have concerns about the library, we can replace these checks with manually maintained lists without changing the overall setup flow. > [!NOTE] > Email validation does not use regex, and addresses like `no@no` are accepted since `.no` is a valid TLD. See: [Stop Validating Email Addresses with Regex](https://davidcel.is/articles/stop-validating-email-addresses-with-regex/). More stricter validation can be added in a follow-up if needed. ### Alternatives Considered 1. **Client-side validation**: would still need a server-side fallback, would require shipping and maintaining disposable/free domain lists in the browser (increasing bundle size), and would not protect direct API callers. 2. **Separate `POST /setup_validate_email` endpoint**: clean separation but adds a new API surface. 3. **Call `/setup_comm_prefs` before `/setup` in the WebUI**: This would preserve the two-call flow, but it requires us to rely on `setup_comm_prefs` being safe to call before initialization, both now and as the setup flow evolves. I chose not to couple the solution to that assumption when we could instead validate and persist comm prefs as part of the setup request itself. 4. **Keep the current API flow and change the WebUI to hold or immediately show credentials**: this keeps the API unchanged, but pushes the problem into frontend state management. Users can still refresh or leave before saving the one-time credentials, so the credential-loss problem is reduced but not eliminated. It also makes the setup flow more complicated and brittle by introducing UI-specific handling for a backend ordering problem. ### Behavior changes (that are worth highlighting) > [!IMPORTANT] > If setup completes without an email, we now explicitly persist comm prefs as missing during `POST /setup_lakefs`. That allows a later `GET /setup_lakefs` call to return `comm_prefs_missing: true` for installs created through this new flow. > > Previously, `POST /setup_lakefs` did not record any comm prefs state. As a result, once setup completed, `GET /setup_lakefs` treated the missing metadata as if comm prefs were already handled and returned `comm_prefs_missing: false`. In practice, that meant the WebUI would not show the follow-up comm prefs step, even though the user had never provided an email. ### Testing Details - **Unit tests (`pkg/api/email_validator_test.go`):** for email validation - **Controller tests (`pkg/api/controller_test.go`):** - **E2E tests (`webui/test/e2e/`):** - Updated fixtures to use `@treeverse.io` instead of `@example.com`. ### Breaking Change? No API surface removed. Additive schema change to `Setup`; `/setup_comm_prefs` remains available. Backward compatible for existing callers. **Behavioral:** Both endpoints now reject invalid/disposable/free emails where they previously accepted them. ### Screenshots **Invalid/Disposable email**: **Free Email**: ### How to Review Commit by commit: - [2c2666a97](https://github.com/treeverse/lakeFS/commit/2c2666a97?w=1) main changes to review. - [f2f13d6c9](https://github.com/treeverse/lakeFS/commit/f2f13d6c9?w=1) `make gen clients` - [5eab800ce](https://github.com/treeverse/lakeFS/commit/5eab800ce?w=1) add backend coverage and table-driven tests for email validation. - [5954e5593](https://github.com/treeverse/lakeFS/commit/5954e5593?w=1) add webui tests for setup page.

title: lakectl fs upload - Multipart upload - generate presigned URL per part
body:
Uploading large files (tried with 25GB) via lakectl fs upload fails.
Encountered 2 issues:
1. API partNumber was limited to 1000 (but for 25GB we have 5000 parts)
   Fix: Raise the partNumber limit in the API spec from 1,000 to 10,000 to match S3's actual maximum.
2. All presigned URLs for multipart upload parts are generated upfront with 15 minutes expiration. With 5MB parts, later parts expire before they're uploaded.
    Fix: for each part, check if the presigned URL is expired. if so - regenerate it.

Fix [#979](https://github.com/treeverse/product/issues/979) 
Fix #10295

body:
Add header-based model routing to LLMInferenceService HTTPRoute rules, enabling shared-gateway deployments where a single gateway dispatches to multiple models by the `X-Gateway-Model-Name` header (header name is configurable).

This header can be injected with components like llm-d BBR (aka IPP) or Gateway-specific native features.

Expand LoRA adapter matches dynamically in Go code after template processing so that adapter requests route through the same backend as the base model.

Key changes:
- Add model-based routing rules to the HTTPRoute template alongside existing path-based rules
- Add expandLoRAAdapterMatches to generate per-adapter header matches
- Add preferPathBasedURL for backward-compatible status.url selection
- Add address type names with -model-routing suffix for discovery
- Add e2e tests for model-based routing across /v1/completions, /v1/chat/completions, and /v1/models endpoints
- Fix extra_headers bug in wait_for_model_response where merged headers were discarded before the HTTP call

Follow ups:
- routing status update, after https://github.com/kserve/kserve/pull/5417 and https://github.com/kserve/kserve/pull/5414

```release-note
Add model-based routing support for LLMInferenceService, enabling shared-gateway deployments where a single gateway dispatches requests to multiple models using the `X-Gateway-Model-Name` header. LoRA adapter requests are automatically routed through the same backend as the base model by dynamically expanding header matches for each adapter defined in `.spec.model.lora.adapters`.

title: ✨ Put cluster-api behind kube-apiserver aggregation layer
body:
## Summary

Moves the cluster-api off its standalone bearer-token auth surface and behind the Kubernetes API aggregation layer. Both the desktop and in-cluster dashboard proxies now route cluster-api requests through kube-apiserver, which authenticates the caller and forwards a verified identity to the cluster-api via the front-proxy headers. The cluster-api consumes that identity and impersonates it on every downstream Kubernetes call, so RBAC is enforced as the originating user rather than as a shared service identity.

## Key Changes

- **cluster-api as an APIService:** routes are mounted under `/apis/api.kubetail.com/v1`, with Kubernetes API discovery and OpenAPI v2/v3 endpoints; Tilt registers the APIService for aggregation.
- **Aggregation auth:** new middleware verifies the front-proxy client cert against the CA loaded from the kube-apiserver ConfigMap, extracts user/groups/extras, and populates `K8SImpersonateCtxKey` on the request context. mTLS (`client-ca-file` + `client-auth-type`) is built from the cluster-api HTTPS settings.
- **Impersonating round tripper:** downstream Kubernetes calls in the cluster-api use the pod ServiceAccount as the bearer and attach `Impersonate-User` / `Impersonate-Group` / `Impersonate-Extra-*` headers from the request identity. The SAR cache is keyed per impersonated identity.
- **Dashboard proxies:** the desktop proxy strips client auth headers and lets the kubeconfig-based `kubectl/proxy` handler authenticate to kube-apiserver as the user; the in-cluster proxy rewrites to the APIService path on kube-apiserver and forwards the session user's bearer.
- **Cleanup:** drops the legacy bearer-token API surface, `X-Forwarded-Authorization` plumbing, and the unused download-token path now that aggregation owns auth.

## Checklist

- [x] Add the correct emoji to the PR title
- [ ] Related issue linked above, if any
- [x] Commit messages use [conventional commit](https://www.conventionalcommits.org) format
- [x] Changes are minimal and focused
author: amorey

title: feat: add per-application driver PodDisruptionBudget (#2949)
body:
## Purpose of this PR

Adds an opt-in per-`SparkApplication` driver PodDisruptionBudget (PDB) so
operators can prevent voluntary disruption (node drains, autoscaler
evictions) of long-running drivers without resorting to cluster-wide
policy.

The feature is gated on two switches that both default to off, so the
change is a no-op for installations that do not opt in:

1. A controller-level flag, `--enable-driver-pdb` (chart value
   `controller.driverPodDisruptionBudget.enable`), gives cluster operators
   a single permission point.
2. A per-application field, `spec.driverPodDisruptionBudget` (`*bool`),
   lets app authors opt in once the operator gate is open.

When both gates are true, the controller maintains a `policy/v1`
PodDisruptionBudget with `minAvailable: 1` and a `matchLabels` selector
that targets the driver pod by name. The PDB is owner-referenced to the
`SparkApplication` so it is GC'd at app deletion, and the reconcile loop
also cleans it up when the app reaches a terminal state or when the spec
is flipped back off.

PDB existence is converged on every non-terminal reconcile (Submitted and
Running state handlers), so:

- Transient API errors during PDB create are retried via standard
  controller-runtime backoff.
- An operator restart with `--enable-driver-pdb` turned on retroactively
  creates PDBs for already-running apps that opted in.
- Flipping `spec.driverPodDisruptionBudget` from false to true (or true
  to false) on a running app takes effect on the next reconcile cycle.

**Proposed changes:**

- API (`api/v1beta2`): add `SparkApplicationSpec.DriverPodDisruptionBudget *bool`,
  regenerate CRDs, deepcopy, OpenAPI, Python API, and chart CRDs.
- Controller (`internal/controller/sparkapplication`): new `driver_pdb.go`
  with idempotent `ensureDriverPDB` / `createDriverPDB` / `deleteDriverPDB`
  helpers; `ensureDriverPDB` wired into the Submitted and Running reconcile
  branches; PDB cleanup in `cleanUpOnTermination`.
- Controller flag: `--enable-driver-pdb` (default false), plumbed through
  `cmd/operator/controller` into `Reconciler.Options.EnableDriverPDB`.
- RBAC: add `policy/poddisruptionbudgets` create/get/list/watch/update/patch/delete
  to the controller role (Helm and Kustomize, in sync).
- Helm chart: `controller.driverPodDisruptionBudget.enable` value, gated
  RBAC rule, and helper template that emits the `--enable-driver-pdb`
  flag when set. Helm-unittest specs cover the on/off matrix.
- Tests: 13 envtest specs in `driver_pdb_test.go` covering both gates,
  spec-flip lifecycle, terminal-state cleanup, OwnerReferences GC, and
  reconcile-loop convergence after operator restart or spec flip; one
  Ginkgo e2e spec in `test/e2e/pdb_test.go` exercising the happy path
  on Kind.
- Tooling: two Makefile fixes that the new e2e suite depends on -
  `make deploy` loads the Spark runtime image into Kind alongside the
  operator image, and `make e2e-test` creates the Kind cluster on demand
  instead of assuming one exists.

## Change Category

- [ ] Bugfix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [x] Feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that could affect existing functionality)
- [ ] Documentation update

### Rationale

A missing-driver event (node drain, autoscaler eviction, kubelet upgrade)
restarts the entire Spark application from scratch. For long-running
drivers this is an expensive failure mode that PDBs were designed to
prevent, but cluster-wide PDB policy is too blunt - most users want PDBs
only for opt-in drivers, not for every Spark driver in the cluster.

The two-gate design (operator flag plus per-app field) keeps the cluster
operator in control of whether the feature exists at all, while letting
individual app authors opt their long-running pipelines in. Defaulting
both gates to off avoids any behavior change for installations that do
not opt in.

Reconcile-loop convergence (rather than a one-shot create on submit)
keeps PDB state correct across operator restarts, transient API failures,
and spec edits - a missing PDB silently defeats the entire feature, so
the controller errs on the side of repeatedly ensuring it exists.

## Checklist

- [x] I have conducted a self-review of my own code.
- [x] I have updated documentation accordingly.
- [x] I have added tests that prove my changes are effective or that my feature works.
- [x] Existing unit tests pass locally with my changes.

### Additional Notes

- All generated artifacts (CRDs in `config/crd/bases` and
  `charts/spark-operator-chart/crds`, deepcopy, OpenAPI, Python API,
  api-docs.md) are committed. `make detect-crds-drift`,
  `make verify-codegen`, `make manifests`, `make helm-unittest`,
  `make kustomize-lint`, `make go-lint`, and `make unit-test` all pass.
- The feature is split across five commits: API + codegen, operator
  code, tests, and two Makefile chores that the e2e suite depends on.
- The e2e spec was verified manually on Kind with `DEPLOY_METHOD=helm`.

---
